one_line_takeaway: A white-box geometry/synonym-substitution attack benchmark showing decoder-only LLMs (OPT, Llama) are much more robust than encoder-decoder T5, that scaling generally helps robustness, and that instruction tuning surprisingly hurts it while LoRA and quantization precision are roughly robustness-neutral.
key_finding: Decoder-only causal LMs (OPT, Llama) are markedly more robust to synonym-substitution attacks than the encoder-decoder T5 family, which shows much higher ASR and replacement rate. Robustness generally improves (ASR falls) with model size, but nonlinearly and with notable fluctuations on MRPC and SST-2 for T5. Three LLM-specific results stand out: (a) instruction tuning HURTS robustness (Flan-T5 has consistently higher ASR than plain T5 on IMDB), hypothesized because instruction tuning made the model better understand the task, letting the attacker locate influential words more easily; (b) quantization precision (fp16/int8/int4) has no consistent effect on robustness; (c) LoRA does not degrade robustness (ASR/replacement essentially unchanged vs full setup), so it gives optimization savings 'for free'. Adding a classification head raises accuracy but also raises ASR (more vulnerable), attributed to DeepFool finding attack directions more easily on the last-layer FFN.
core_contribution: An empirical study assessing the adversarial robustness of three families of open-source LLMs (T5, OPT, Llama) under a white-box, gradient-based synonym-substitution attack. The authors adapt a geometry-inspired word-substitution attack (extending Meng & Wattenhofer's geometry attack and using DeepFool for candidate selection) so that it can attack fine-tuned LLMs by leveraging output logits and generation-loss gradients. Their claimed contributions are: (1) a 'white-box style' attack that uses logits and gradients to expose vulnerabilities; (2) a robustness benchmark organized around LLM training strategies (LoRA, quantization/precision, instruction tuning, classification-head vs prompt tuning); and (3) evaluation across five text-classification datasets. The distinctive angle vs. prior robustness studies (which they position as inference-only, e.g. PromptBench, DecodingTrust) is that they participate in the model's fine-tuning and attack the model's actual weights/gradients rather than only its prompts.
threat_model: White-box adversary with full access to the target model's weights, architecture, output logits, and gradients (and who participates in fine-tuning). The attacker performs iterative word-level synonym substitution on the input text of a classification task, choosing replacement words that maximize the projection of the embedding delta onto the generation-loss gradient (geometry attack + DeepFool candidate set filtered by cosine-similarity threshold epsilon), with the goal of flipping the classifier's prediction while preserving semantic similarity. This is an evaluation/robustness-probing threat model rather than a deployed exploit; the paper's own ethics statement flags that such adversarial generation could produce harmful/misleading text.
limitations: Scope is limited to text classification only (the authors note generation tasks are left to future work). The 'novel white-box style attack' is largely an adaptation/application of an existing geometry-inspired attack (Meng & Wattenhofer) plus DeepFool candidate selection, so novelty is more in the systematic LLM-technique benchmark than in the attack primitive. Llama is only evaluated at two sizes (7b, 13b), so its scaling conclusions are weak ('differences minimal'). The attack is a single perturbation type (word-level synonym substitution); no character/sentence-level or prompt-injection/jailbreak threats are covered. Results contain internal inconsistencies (e.g. T5-11b IMDB numbers differ between Table 3 Acc 0.9122/ASR 0.6604 and Table 8 Acc 0.9348/ASR 0.4752) and several 'anomalous' trend reversals (OPT-13b on MRPC) that are noted but not fully explained. No adversarial defense/training is proposed or evaluated.

datasets_used: IMDB (binary sentiment; 25,000 train / 25,000 test; avg length 279.48)
SST-2 (binary sentiment; 67,300 train / 1,820 test; avg length 19.81)
MRPC (binary paraphrase; 3,670 train / 1,730 test; avg length 52.89)
AGNews (4-class news; 120,000 train / 7,600 test; avg length 43.93)
quantitative_results: IMDB ASR: T5-60m 0.8491 vs OPT-13b 0.1500 vs Llama-13b 0.1304 (Table 3), showing T5 far more vulnerable than decoder-only models
IMDB Acc/attack: T5-60m 0.1256 vs Llama-13b 0.8237 (Table 3), i.e. Llama retains most accuracy under attack while T5 collapses
SST-2 highest clean Acc T5-11b 0.9656 but its ASR 0.7672 is not the lowest in the T5 series; T5 ASR minimum at T5-770m (Table 4)
DBpedia (14-class) clean Acc is very high across models (T5-770m 0.9921, Llama-13b 0.9921), with T5 ASR dropping sharply from 0.5952 (60m) to 0.1113 (11b) (Table 7)

Instruction tuning: Flan-T5 shows higher ASR than plain T5 on IMDB (e.g. T5-220m ASR 0.9463 vs Flan-T5-220m 0.8862 is lower, but Flan-T5-60m 0.8968 > T5-60m 0.8491 and Flan-T5-3b 0.7489 > T5-3b 0.6436), supporting the 'instruction tuning hurts robustness' claim (Table 8)
Precision ablation IMDB: T5-770m ASR fp16 0.8208 / int8 0.8312 / int4 0.8127 and OPT-1.3b ASR fp16 0.1868 / int8 0.1859 / int4 0.1820 — no consistent precision effect (Table 9)
LoRA ablation IMDB: OPT-1.3b ASR 0.1847 (no LoRA) vs 0.1859 (LoRA); T5-770m 0.8347 vs 0.8312 — essentially unchanged (Table 10)
Classification head IMDB: OPT-125m ASR rises from 0.2297 (headless) to 0.3151 (with head) though Acc rises 0.8616 to 0.9074 — head increases both accuracy and vulnerability (Table 11)
baselines_compared: Cross-family comparison: T5 (encoder-decoder) vs OPT (decoder-only) vs Llama (decoder-only)
Within-family model-size scaling: T5 60m/220m/770m/3b/11b; OPT 125m/350m/1.3b/2.7b/6.7b/13b; Llama 7b/13b
Instruction tuning: standard T5 vs Flan-T5 (same sizes)
Precision settings: fp16 vs int8 vs int4 (T5-770m, OPT-1.3b)
Fine-tuning method: with vs without LoRA (T5-770m, OPT-1.3b, OPT-2.7b)
Architecture: OPT with vs without a classification head
Positioned against prior inference-only robustness benchmarks cited as related work (PromptBench, DecodingTrust/AdvGLUE++, On the Robustness of ChatGPT) rather than run as head-to-head baselines
